blurb: A towering native prairie sunflower — 6 to 10 feet of arching stems lined top to bottom with golden, dark-eyed flowers in fall, mobbed by bees and stripped for seed by goldfinches. Give it room at the back of the border.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
spread: Clumping but spreads by rhizomes into colonies — give it room or divide to contain
seasons: Tall leafy stems through summer; golden dark-eyed flowers lining the upper stems in late summer and fall; seedheads feed birds into winter
wildlife: Major fall nectar for bees and butterflies; oil-rich seeds heavily used by goldfinches and other birds; larval host
toxic: Non-toxic; the seeds and tubers are edible (see Edible parts)
